g.data-narration: Welcome to Handling Multiple Devices - a key topic in Computer Organization about efficient CPU-peripheral communication.
g.text: Handling Multiple Devices in Computer Organization
Efficient I/O Management for High-Performance Computing

g.data-narration: The core problem: CPUs execute billions of instructions per second while peripherals like disks take milliseconds - a massive speed gap.
g.text: ⚠ THE PROBLEM: CPU vs I/O Speed Mismatch

g.data-narration: We solve this with four techniques: Programmed I/O, Interrupt-Driven I/O, DMA, and I/O Processors - each more efficient than the last.
g.text: 📋 SOLUTION OVERVIEW: Evolution of I/O Techniques

g.data-narration: Programmed I/O is the simplest but most inefficient method - the CPU sits idle, polling the device status until the operation completes.
Interrupt-driven I/O lets the CPU work on other tasks while the device prepares data, then the device interrupts the CPU when ready.
When multiple devices interrupt simultaneously, priority systems determine which gets serviced first using daisy chains or interrupt controllers.
Direct Memory Access uses a DMA controller to transfer data directly between devices and memory, bypassing the CPU for large transfers.
g.text: 3️⃣ DIRECT MEMORY ACCESS (DMA) - For Bulk Transfers

g.data-narration: The CPU programs the DMA controller with source, destination addresses and transfer size, then the DMA handles the entire block transfer autonomously.
I/O Processors are specialized CPUs with their own instruction set that execute channel programs independently, providing maximum parallelism.
Let's trace a print job: CPU initiates printing, continues working, printer interrupts when ready, CPU sends more data - all without waiting.
Compare the methods: Programmed I/O wastes CPU time, interrupts improve efficiency, DMA handles bulk transfers, and IOPs maximize parallelism.
Key points: Overcome CPU-I/O speed gap, interrupts enable async operation, priority ensures critical devices first, DMA bypasses CPU, IOPs maximize parallelism.
In summary: I/O techniques evolved from polling to interrupt-driven to DMA to I/O processors, each offering greater efficiency and complexity for modern computing.
This knowledge is essential for designing SoCs, real-time systems, and high-performance computing infrastructure - foundation for advanced topics like multicore processing.
g.text: • Critical for real-time systems and embedded computing
• Foundation for parallel processing and multicore system design
